title: Reeb Orbits Frequently Intersecting a Symplectic Surface
speaker: Michael Hutchings
abstract: Given a local system on a complex algebraic variety, what are the subvarieties on which the monodromy drops? The talk will discuss these monodromy special loci, a natural generalisation of (the positive period dimension components of) the Hodge loci.

proof_stages.label: Contact/Reeb preliminaries and motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a closed connected contact three-manifold, its contact volume, Reeb vector field and periodic-orbit action, then recalls existence, multiplicity, density and equidistribution results to frame the question.

proof_stages.label: Admissible symplectic surfaces and niceness
proof_stages.summary: An admissible symplectic surface is introduced as an almost-embedded oriented surface positively transverse to the Reeb flow in its interior and with Reeb-orbit boundary, together with a non-generic rotational local model near each boundary orbit called niceness.

proof_stages.label: Frequency theorem and interior-orbit corollary
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that the supremum of the intersection frequency gamma dot Sigma divided by the Reeb action is at least area(Sigma)/vol(Y,lambda), and the boundary-exclusion corollary says that if all boundary orbits have smaller frequency then some non-boundary orbit intersects the surface interior.

proof_stages.label: Generic equidistribution benchmark
proof_stages.summary: Irie's generic equidistribution theorem would imply the same lower bound by applying it to a thin Reeb-flow slab over the surface, so the new content is obtaining the result without genericity.

proof_stages.label: Calabi and mean-action application
proof_stages.summary: For an area-preserving disk map with fixed boundary rotation, an associated open-book contact form on S^3 translates return times, intersections and contact volume into the action function and Calabi invariant, so the corollary recovers the mean-action inequality.

proof_stages.label: Spectral invariants and alternative ECH capacities
proof_stages.summary: The proof uses elementary spectral invariants with spectrality, C^0-continuity and a Weyl law, together with four-dimensional alternative ECH capacities, their capacity inequality, and their own Weyl asymptotics.

proof_stages.label: Controlled bump perturbation near the surface
proof_stages.summary: The contact form is multiplied by a carefully chosen symmetric bump near Sigma, increasing volume by roughly 2 delta area(Sigma) and giving a bijection of Reeb orbits away from the boundary whose actions increase by delta times the intersection number, with niceness controlling the boundary region.

proof_stages.label: Weyl-law contradiction from a frequency upper bound
proof_stages.summary: Assuming all intersection frequencies are bounded by F gives a differential estimate for spectral invariants, and combining its integrated form with the capacity inequality and Weyl laws forces F to be at least area(Sigma)/vol(Y,lambda), proving the theorem.

examples.name: Generic global surfaces of section
examples.note: Global surfaces of section are cited as familiar instances of admissible symplectic surfaces with the extra property that every Reeb trajectory eventually hits the surface, making the definition concrete rather than driving the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites generic global surfaces of section as examples of admissible symplectic surfaces and states their defining hitting property, but does not work through a particular one

examples.name: Projected ECH U-map holomorphic curves
examples.note: For nondegenerate contact forms, holomorphic curves counted by the ECH U-map may project to almost embedded surfaces with Reeb-orbit ends, giving another optional source of admissible surfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes projected ECH U-map holomorphic curves as a possible source of admissible surfaces if the projection is embedded, without analyzing a concrete curve

examples.name: Irrational ellipsoid with a p,q torus-cylinder surface
examples.note: The irrational ellipsoid has only two simple Reeb orbits, and the constructed cylinder has boundary on them so that the boundary frequencies exactly equal area divided by volume while no orbit meets the interior, illustrating sharpness and the need for the boundary-orbit hypothesis.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the Reeb vector field and periods on the irrational ellipsoid, constructs the p,q torus-cylinder, computes its boundary, intersection frequencies, area, and volume, and checks the theorem is sharp

passes.reason: The toric irrational ellipsoid/cylinder example exactly realizes the frequency bound and shows a stronger interior-intersection conclusion can fail without the extra boundary hypothesis.
The irrational ellipsoid toric cylinder is a highly regular sharp-equality case with no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits, showing the stronger interior-orbit claim can fail.
The irrational ellipsoid toric-cylinder example realizes the theorem’s bound sharply and shows that an unconditional interior-intersection statement can fail.
The toric irrational ellipsoid example realizes the intersection-frequency bound exactly and shows that without the extra boundary hypothesis no interior-intersecting Reeb orbit need exist.
The toric irrational ellipsoid with the constant-slope cylinder sharply realizes the bound and is used to show the theorem may see only boundary orbits with no interior intersections.
The toric constant-slope ellipsoid model is highly regular and attains the frequency bound, and is used to show interior-intersecting Reeb orbits need not exist.

the toric irrational ellipsoid with constant-slope cylinder is a sharp equality case where only the boundary Reeb orbits realize the bound, showing interior-intersecting orbits need not exist without the extra hypothesis.

the toric irrational ellipsoid with the p,q cylinder is a sharp equality case whose only relevant Reeb orbits are boundary orbits, showing an interior-intersection conclusion can fail without extra hypotheses

The toric irrational ellipsoid gives a tight case where the boundary orbits exactly realize the bound and no interior-intersecting Reeb orbits exist, blocking a stronger interior-orbit claim.

The irrational ellipsoid with the p,q torus-cylinder is a toric sharp case whose boundary orbits attain the bound and shows that no interior-intersecting Reeb orbit need exist without the extra hypothesis.

examples.name: Unit cotangent bundles and geodesic intersections
examples.note: As a prospective source of admissible surfaces if the niceness condition could be removed, unit cotangent bundles of surfaces would translate the theorem into statements about how frequently geodesics intersect.
examples.engagement_reason: unit cotangent bundles and geodesic-based surfaces are only raised as potential examples in an open question about geodesic intersections, with no concrete construction or computation
